---
title: "Tutorial 1: Simple case"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: false
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
lightbox: true
reference-location: margin
freeze: auto
cache: true
conda:
    environment: easywhisper
resources:
    - ../_assets/tale_two_cities_ch01.txt
    - ../_assets/taleoftwocities_01_dickens_64kb_trimmed.json
---

This tutorial will demonstrate the most straightforward case for aligning text and audio. It assumes we have access to a transcript that corresponds to everything spoken in the audio.  

For the tutorial, we will be using:

* An audio recording of the first chapter of "A Tale of Two Cities", read by Paul Adams ([LibriVox](https://librivox.org/a-tale-of-two-cities-by-charles-dickens-2/)).
* The corresponding ebook [text](https://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/98/pg98.txt) from [Project Gutenberg](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/98).  

`easyaligner` can handle some extra speech at the beginning and end of the audio with its `start_wildcard` and `end_wildcard` arguments. For best results, however, the transcript should cover as much of the spoken audio as possible.

::: {.callout-note title="Scenario"}
A text transcript that covers (almost) everything that is spoken in the audio.

![](../_assets/scenario_full_coverage.svg){width="100%"}
:::

## Download text and audio

Let's begin with downloading the book from Project Gutenberg, and then apply a regular expression to extract the first chapter's text.

```{python}
import re
import requests

url = "https://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/98/pg98.txt"
full_text = requests.get(url).text

# Extract Chapter I text (i.e. the text between "CHAPTER I. The Period" and "CHAPTER II.")
match = re.search(
    r"(?<=CHAPTER I\.\r\nThe Period\r\n)[\s\S]+?(?=CHAPTER II\.)",
    full_text,
)
text = match.group().strip()
```

Before we move on to forced alignment, here's the text of the first chapter for reference. Note especially the paragraph breaks and punctuation. One of `easyaligner`'s main features is that it will retain the original text's formatting after alignment. 

::: {.transcript-card .transcript-container}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(text)
```
:::

Next, we download the audio of the first chapter and save it to disk.

```{python}
#| output: false
from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download

snapshot_download(
    "Lauler/easytranscriber_tutorials",
    repo_type="dataset",
    local_dir="data/tutorials",
    allow_patterns="tale-of-two-cities_short-en/*",
)
```

Here's the audio we'll be aligning with the text:

<div class="audio-card">
  <div class="audio-card-label">Sample audio</div>
  <div class="audio-card-title"><em>A Tale of Two Cities</em> — Chapter 1 (LibriVox)</div>
  <audio controls>
    <source src="https://huggingface.co/datasets/Lauler/easytranscriber_tutorials/resolve/main/tale-of-two-cities_short-en/taleoftwocities_01_dickens_64kb_trimmed.mp3"
    type="audio/mpeg">
  </audio>
</div>

## Align text and audio

The process for aligning the text and audio looks similar to the simple example in the [overview](overview.qmd) section. This time around, however, we will be aligning the entire first chapter of A Tale of Two Cities, as opposed to only the first paragraph. 

```{python}
# | output: false
from pathlib import Path

from transformers import (
    AutoModelForCTC,
    Wav2Vec2Processor,
)

from easyaligner.text import load_tokenizer
from easyaligner.data.datamodel import SpeechSegment
from easyaligner.pipelines import pipeline
from easyaligner.text import text_normalizer
from easyaligner.vad.pyannote import load_vad_model

text = text.strip()

# The alignments will be organized according to how the text is tokenized
tokenizer = load_tokenizer(language="english")  # sentence tokenizer
span_list = list(tokenizer.span_tokenize(text))  # start, end character indices for each sentence
speeches = [[SpeechSegment(speech_id=0, text=text, text_spans=span_list, start=None, end=None)]]

# Load models and run pipeline
model_vad = load_vad_model()
model = AutoModelForCTC.from_pretrained("facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h").to("cuda").half()
processor = Wav2Vec2Processor.from_pretrained("facebook/wav2vec2-base-960h")

# File(s) to align
audio_dir = "data/tutorials/tale-of-two-cities_short-en"
audio_files = [file.name for file in Path(audio_dir).glob("*")]

pipeline(
    vad_model=model_vad,
    emissions_model=model,
    processor=processor,
    audio_paths=audio_files,
    audio_dir=audio_dir,
    speeches=speeches,
    alignment_strategy="speech",
    text_normalizer_fn=text_normalizer,
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    start_wildcard=True,
    end_wildcard=True,
    blank_id=processor.tokenizer.pad_token_id,
    word_boundary="|",
)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
A list of suitable emission models for different languages can be found in the [WhisperX library](https://github.com/m-bain/whisperX/blob/d8a078eed46b139f90d7f33b472119e9be9ff969/whisperx/alignment.py#L41-L77).

If your audio and transcript are multilingual, you can try using the Massively Multilingual Speech model from Meta: [mms-1b-all](https://huggingface.co/facebook/mms-1b-all). 
:::

## Result: Force aligned output

The text transcript below the audio player is highlighted in sync with the words spoken in the audio.

<div id="audio-player" class="audio-card">
  <div class="audio-card-label">Sample audio</div>
  <div class="audio-card-title"><em>A Tale of Two Cities</em> — Chapter 1 (LibriVox)</div>
  <audio controls>
    <source src="https://huggingface.co/datasets/Lauler/easytranscriber_tutorials/resolve/main/tale-of-two-cities_short-en/taleoftwocities_01_dickens_64kb_trimmed.mp3"
    type="audio/mpeg">
  </audio>
</div>

<div id="transcript-container" class="transcript-container transcript-card" data-highlight-sentence="true" data-src="../_assets/taleoftwocities_01_dickens_64kb_trimmed.json"></div>

::: {.column-margin}
::: {.callout-tip}
You can click anywhere in the text to jump to that point in the audio. The text is also highlighted when you drag the audio slider!
:::
:::

<script src="demo.js"></script>
